blank_answer: watching from a distance
letter_text: I sit by the window often these days. I look outside, then inside, then outside again, and time just goes by. If someone calls my name, I answer. If no one calls, I just stay there. I'm not sure if this is laziness or rest. How is it for you?

blank_answer: keeping a distance
letter_text: Last week I heard that an old friend had died. I didn't go to the funeral. It seemed like the right thing to do, but my body wouldn't move. I'm not sad even now. That is what feels stranger. I don't know what's normal anymore.

blank_answer: it is quiet
letter_text: When I'm in a crowded place, I find a corner. I'm not doing it on purpose, but somehow I end up standing there. If someone speaks to me, I answer briefly. I could say more, but I cut it short. Sometimes I envy the ones who keep talking.

blank_answer: those days
letter_text: Some days just pass through me. I can't really remember what happened. I look at photos and I must have been there, but the feeling of that day doesn't come back. If someone brings it up, I just say 'oh, right, that.' It's all gone.

blank_answer: fine
letter_text: I'm fine. Really, I'm fine. People keep saying I don't look fine, and that's starting to bother me. I know my own state best. I'm just tired. A few nights of sleep and it'll pass.

blank_answer: drift somewhere else
letter_text: In some moments, I feel like I'm not there. My body is, but my mind is somewhere else. I was in a meeting once, and when it ended I couldn't remember what was said. Everyone told me I was listening well. I don't know if that's frightening or comforting.

blank_answer: the feelings, for later
letter_text: I have to finish the work first. I'll deal with the feelings later, I tell myself, but 'later' doesn't come. Late at night, when I'm alone, something rises for a moment, and then I sleep. By morning there's another task. Maybe this is what becoming an adult means.

blank_answer: analyze why
letter_text: We broke up. I thought about why for several days. Our patterns, attachment styles, our childhoods — I have it all sorted out. When it's sorted out, I get calmer. A friend asked, 'so are you sad?' I said, 'I'm not sure about that part yet.'

blank_answer: let things pass
letter_text: Lately, the way other people get excited feels far away to me. I can't quite follow what they're so happy or angry about. I'm with them, but it doesn't feel like I'm with them. I'm not sure if I changed or they did.

blank_answer: look only at the facts
letter_text: The test results came back. They aren't good. The doctor explained things calmly, and I listened calmly. I took notes. I came home and explained the same way to my family. They cried. I didn't. A few days later, in the bathroom, I cried for a moment. Once.

blank_answer: the news without the weight
letter_text: I told three people today about something difficult, and each time I sounded almost cheerful. They thanked me for being strong. Strong isn't quite the word. The words came out one way and the feeling stayed somewhere else, in a different room with the door closed.

author_pseudonym: the one with the radio on
blank_answer: the radio
letter_text: I leave the radio on while I cook, while I drive, before I sleep. Silence is when something almost-shaped tries to come up. I don't know what it is. I don't want to find out tonight either. Maybe one day I'll let the room be quiet.

blank_answer: go heavy in the chest with no warning
letter_text: Most days are fine. Then in the middle of something ordinary — buying eggs, waiting for the kettle — my chest goes heavy and I don't know why. By the time I notice, it's already half gone. I keep meaning to ask someone what that is.

author_pseudonym: the one with the lights on
blank_answer: every light in the house
letter_text: I leave every light in the house on. I sleep better that way. People keep asking if something happened and I say no, I just like the brightness. They don't believe me. I'm not sure what to do with that disbelief, so I turn another lamp on.

blank_answer: doesn't have a sad story
letter_text: People keep trying to find a sad story in me. I don't have one. I really don't. Something happened, yes, but it's not the kind of thing that ruins a person, and I'm not ruined. I wish they'd believe me when I say I'm okay.

blank_answer: lose the drive home
letter_text: I drove home from work yesterday and I don't remember any of it. The car is in the driveway. The keys are on the counter. That hour of the afternoon is just missing. It happens more often than I'd like to admit. I haven't told anyone.

blank_answer: I can step behind my own eyes
letter_text: In long meetings I notice I've been gone, and the world continued without me. I nodded at the right places. Someone asked if I had thoughts and I gave a sentence that fit. I don't know where I went. I'm starting to want to know.

blank_answer: a quiet shelf for hard things
letter_text: I keep a quiet shelf in my head for hard things. Not forever — only until Friday evening, until I get home from the hospital, until the meeting is over. Then I take things down. Most of them come back. Some don't, and I'm not sure if they're handled or just gone.

blank_answer: save Sunday mornings for what I pushed aside
letter_text: I've made Sunday mornings a ritual for letting the week land. Coffee, a notebook, the chair by the window. I sit and feel what scared me and what I'd been pushing aside. I usually end heavier and lighter at the same time. I think that's what it's supposed to feel like.

blank_answer: things have a name and a cause
letter_text: I stay in rooms where things have a name and a cause. If you tell me what's happening, neurologically, structurally, historically — I can be there. Rooms without those, where someone is sobbing and there's no clean reason, I leave faster than I should. I'm working on staying.

blank_answer: explain what was probably happening instead of how it felt
letter_text: When my therapist asks how I felt, I usually explain what was probably happening neurologically. She lets me, then asks again, gently. The second time the answer is shorter. The third time it's just a word. We're getting somewhere, I think.

author_pseudonym: the one with the short fuse
blank_answer: say things before I know I mean them
letter_text: I open my mouth and the words are already out. By the time I hear them, it's too late. The other person flinches, and I flinch too, on the inside. I tell myself I'll wait next time. Sometimes I do. Most times the fuse is lit before I notice it.

author_pseudonym: the one who slammed the door
blank_answer: a door I shouldn't have slammed
letter_text: I left the apartment last night without finishing the sentence. I walked twelve blocks before I noticed I'd been crying. By the time I came back, the whole shape of the argument had changed — neither of us could remember what started it. I'm tired of letting my body speak before I do.

blank_answer: heat that carries from the morning into the afternoon
letter_text: I had a hard call before lunch and was sharp with the cashier by two. I knew while I was doing it. I couldn't stop in time. I went back to the same store the next day and bought something small from her. She was kind about it. I don't think she remembered.

author_pseudonym: the one who calls a friend
blank_answer: call someone before deciding what I think
letter_text: When something hard happens, my hand reaches for the phone before I reach for an opinion. I want to hear another voice in the room. Sometimes I call three people in a row. By the third call I usually know what I think — or I've stopped needing to.

blank_answer: I'm in a room with the people I trust
letter_text: I feel most like myself in small groups of people I've known for years. Not at parties. Around a kitchen table, with the dishes still out, when the conversation has gone quiet for a minute and no one is rushing to fill it. That's the closest I get to rest.

blank_answer: would rather not carry it alone
letter_text: When I find out something hard, I want to tell someone within the hour. I used to think this was weakness. Now I think it's how I metabolize. The thing gets smaller as soon as it's in another person's ear. Maybe that's not maturity, but it keeps me upright.

blank_answer: shows up when someone is sick
letter_text: I drove four hours last weekend to sit with a friend after her surgery. I made soup, walked her dog, didn't talk much. I came home tired and clearer than I'd been in months. I'm not sure if that's generosity or something else, but it's the realest I've felt all year.

blank_answer: find a way to be useful when I don't know what else to do
letter_text: When my own problems get too big to look at, I turn toward someone else's — a neighbor's leaking sink, a coworker overwhelmed by a deadline. I fix what I can. I come back to my own life smaller and lighter. I'm trying to make sure I come back to it.

blank_answer: what I needed once and didn't get
letter_text: I notice I tend to give the things I needed when I was younger and didn't receive — patience to anxious students, time to friends going through divorces, a steady ear to anyone who's just lost someone. It costs me less than it should. Maybe because I know exactly what's missing.

blank_answer: rehearse hard conversations the night before
letter_text: Before any conversation I'm dreading, I run it three times in the shower. What I'll say. What they might say. What I'll say back. The real version is never the rehearsed one, but I'm calmer for having tried. Some friends find this strange. It keeps me from going silent at the table.

author_pseudonym: the one with the bag packed
blank_answer: a small bag, in case the news is bad
letter_text: I keep a small bag packed by the door this winter — a change of clothes, a phone charger, the names of the medications. My mother is older than she's ever been. The bag isn't fear exactly. It's more like making the future a little softer for the version of me who'll need it.

blank_answer: tries to feel a thing in advance
letter_text: I let myself feel the loss before it's here, in small doses, so the wave doesn't take me apart when it lands. Some people think this is morbid. For me it's the opposite — I get to have a little grief now, and a little of the day in front of me, instead of one big wall later.

author_pseudonym: the one in the small house
blank_answer: imagine the small house in my head
letter_text: I keep returning to a small house with a field beyond the window. No neighbors. A kettle. A chair. No one knows the address. I don't think I want to live there. I want to know it exists. On hard days I sit in it for an hour and the day softens.

blank_answer: the same daydream
letter_text: I keep going back to the same daydream — a different version of my life where I made one different choice ten years ago. It's getting more detailed. I notice the names of streets in it now. I'm not sure if it's planning or escape. Probably escape. I'm trying to bring some of it into the real day.

blank_answer: the world I made up is kinder
letter_text: When the day gets too loud I drift into a version of it I made up, where the people are kinder and the rooms are quieter. I don't tell anyone I'm doing this. I come back when called. I'm starting to wonder how much time I've spent in there.

author_pseudonym: the one who finds the flaw
blank_answer: notice the flaw before the rest
letter_text: Someone hands me something they made and I see the seam first. A new friend says something slightly off and I catch it. I used to think this was discernment. Now I'm not so sure. I'm trying to look for what's true in a thing before I look for what's wrong.

blank_answer: another second date
letter_text: I left another second date behind last week. By dessert I'd already started cataloging the small ways he wasn't quite right. He was kind. He called the next day. I didn't answer. I'm starting to wonder if I'm protecting myself from something or just from people.

blank_answer: feel almost relieved when something disappoints me
letter_text: When something I was hoping for falls short, there is a small relief underneath the disappointment — like I was right to keep some distance. I don't love that this is true about me. I'm trying to let one thing this month be just good, without me doing this to it.

author_pseudonym: the one who snapped at the cashier
blank_answer: snap at the wrong person
letter_text: I had a hard meeting and was short with the cashier by lunchtime. He'd done nothing. I knew while I was doing it that the heat belonged somewhere else. I went back the next day. He didn't remember me. I remembered him for a week.

blank_answer: an anger I can't quite address
letter_text: I carry an anger I can't properly direct. The person who hurt me is two states away and won't answer. So the kettle gets it. The cabinet gets it. The dog watches from the rug. Most days now I can put it down before I open the door. Most.

blank_answer: find my anger in the wrong room
letter_text: I came home tense from a call with my father and was sharp with my partner about a dish in the sink. They asked, gently, if it was actually about the dish. It wasn't. We sat down. The call came back into the room and we looked at it together. I'm trying to do that earlier.

author_pseudonym: the one who says it's fine
blank_answer: say it's fine, then explain why nothing helps
letter_text: I tell people I'm okay, then I tell them everything that's wrong, then I take apart every solution they offer. By the end they're tired and so am I. I don't know what I want from them. Maybe just for them to sit with me while it stays broken. I'm trying to ask for that directly.

blank_answer: the same complaint, a little altered
letter_text: I keep going back to the same story with my friend. She's tried every angle. I've found a flaw in every angle. She's stopped suggesting things. I miss the suggestions. I think I'd like to let one of them work this week, even imperfectly, and see what happens.

blank_answer: a tiredness that won't take help
letter_text: I'm exhausted by my own exhaustion. People offer rest, time off, doctors, books. I have a reason none of it will work. I watch my friends get smaller with each call. I haven't yet figured out how to stop, but I noticed it this week, which feels like the first half of something.

blank_answer: make a joke first, to make it small enough to hold
letter_text: At the funeral, at the diagnosis, after the bad news — a joke comes out before I know I'm making it. People laugh, mostly. Sometimes I laugh too, and it isn't a lie. The thing gets smaller for a minute. I get to breathe. Then later, when I'm alone, the size comes back, and I let it.

blank_answer: a punchline ready, like a window I can open
letter_text: I carry a punchline ready, the way some people carry a lighter. When a room gets too heavy, I open it. Most of the time the room thanks me. Sometimes the joke lands wrong and I learn something. Lately I've been letting the heavy rooms stay heavy a little longer before I open the window.

author_pseudonym: the one who laughs in waiting rooms
blank_answer: laugh in waiting rooms
letter_text: I laughed quietly in the waiting room before my mother's surgery. Not because anything was funny, exactly — because the alternative was a kind of stiffness I couldn't survive in for two more hours. The nurse smiled. So did my mother, when I told her later. We needed it.

author_pseudonym: the one who falls in love with potential
blank_answer: fall in love with what someone could be
letter_text: I meet someone and within a week I've decided they're rare. The way they listen, the way they read, the small kindness with the waiter. I build a tower out of three details. When it cracks I have to face the actual person, which has always been hard. I'm trying to wait longer before building.

author_pseudonym: the one with the imagined mother
blank_answer: the version of her who would have understood
letter_text: I keep going back to the mother I made up — the one who would have understood, who would have asked the right question, who would have stayed in the room. The real one is more complicated. The imagined one is more comforting. I'm slowly learning to let both exist in the same week.

blank_answer: is always looking for the one who'll save me
letter_text: Every new job, every new friend, every new therapist — I decide quietly that this one might be the one who turns it around. They're not. They couldn't be. I'm beginning to suspect that the work I keep waiting for someone else to do is mine. That suspicion is uncomfortable and quiet and useful.

blank_answer: handles everything
letter_text: I'm the one people call when something breaks — the project, the family crisis, the friend in trouble, the faucet. I don't know what would happen if I dropped one of them. I'm starting to think that the thing I'm afraid of dropping isn't the task. It's the version of me that gets to be the one who handles it.

author_pseudonym: the one with the long list
blank_answer: a long list of what I owe people
letter_text: I carry a list of what I owe people, or what I think I owe them. Calls to return, favors to repay, meals to make. The list never ends. I'm not sure I asked for half of these debts. I'm trying, this month, to take one off without paying it, just to see if the world holds.

blank_answer: I'm the one who holds the center
letter_text: If I let any of it slip, something larger seems like it would come apart. I've been told that isn't true. I'm starting to believe that. The week I was sick, the world kept turning. People I assumed needed me figured it out. It was humbling and a little freeing.

author_pseudonym: the one who agrees and disappears
blank_answer: say yes and then go quiet
letter_text: I agreed to help with the move. Then I forgot to bring the boxes. Then I was late. Then I had to leave early. By the end no one was angry exactly, but I knew. I'd said no with my body all week, while my mouth kept saying yes. I'm trying to let the no out of my mouth instead.

author_pseudonym: the one with the unsent draft
blank_answer: a sentence in a draft email from last spring
letter_text: I left a sentence unsent in a draft from last spring — the thing I couldn't say to my manager. Instead I forwarded the work slightly late, with a polite note. He noticed. He didn't say anything. I noticed he noticed. That whole quiet exchange did more damage than the sentence would have.

blank_answer: a small ledger of things I haven't said
letter_text: I'm fine on the surface. Underneath there's a small ledger of things I haven't said, and when someone senses the cold in my voice and asks if anything's wrong, I say no and hold it tighter. I'm getting tired of carrying the ledger. I might try opening it on a quiet evening this week.

blank_answer: the feeling that I'm being judged
letter_text: I keep going back to the feeling that I'm being judged. I walk into rooms with that weight already there. Last week a friend told me she'd been thinking, the whole dinner, that I seemed warm. I almost didn't believe her. I'm starting to suspect the judging voice is mostly mine.

author_pseudonym: the one with the angry coworker
blank_answer: find anger out there that started in me
letter_text: I was sure my coworker was angry with me all week. He wasn't. He had a sick child and was distracted. The anger I'd been seeing was something I'd buried earlier, and it found a face. When I noticed, the room got softer. I'm trying to look at my own anger before I look for it in others.

blank_answer: is sure people are not on her side
letter_text: I'm sure my new neighbor doesn't like me. I have evidence — the way she nodded, the small pause, the closed window. None of it is evidence, really. I'm slowly learning that what I'm reading on her face is sometimes a sentence I wrote and forgot to sign.

author_pseudonym: the calm one others get edgy around
blank_answer: stays calm while the room gets edgy
letter_text: I'm calm, sometimes too calm, and the people with me start fidgeting and raising their voices. Afterwards I tell myself I had nothing to do with it. I'm not sure that's true anymore. Some of what I'm not feeling seems to land in whoever's nearest. I'd like to start carrying my share.

blank_answer: a quiet panic that isn't only mine
letter_text: I carry a quiet panic that isn't only mine. Whoever I'm near seems to take on the half I'm not holding. My partner gets restless. My friend gets snappy. They don't know why. I'm starting to know why, which is the first step toward not putting it on them.

blank_answer: make others feel what I won't
letter_text: I delivered a hard message in a flat voice and the room turned sharp around me. Someone cried who hadn't cried about their own news. I left the meeting calm and they left it shaken. I'm trying to feel a thing as it goes through me, instead of handing it over.

blank_answer: have good reasons that aren't the real ones
letter_text: I have a clean reason for every decision I made this year. I notice that they're all clean. Real reasons are usually messier than mine. I'm starting to look underneath my best explanations for the smaller, dumber, more honest one. It's usually fear. Sometimes it's comfort.

blank_answer: convince myself first, then everyone else
letter_text: I made a hard call last month and could lay out three reasons it was right by dinner. The reasons held. Years from now I'll probably see the actual reason underneath, which is usually the one I wasn't ready to say out loud. For now the three good reasons are doing their job.

blank_answer: the smoother version of the story
letter_text: I keep going back to the smoother explanation I gave myself because it lets me sleep. The rougher version surfaces sometimes — usually around 3 a.m. — and I usually push it back under. Lately I've been letting it sit on the edge of the bed for a minute before I do.

blank_answer: be extra warm to the people I dislike
letter_text: I noticed I've been bringing the coworker who grates on me coffee, three days running. Laughing at his jokes. Asking after his weekend. None of it is exactly fake. None of it is exactly me, either. I'm trying to figure out what I'd say if I let myself say a smaller, true thing instead.

blank_answer: tell everyone how happy I am for them
letter_text: When my friend's career took off last year, I told everyone how thrilled I was. The volume of my happiness was louder than the feeling could carry. I think I knew. Now, quieter, I can also be a little jealous and still glad for her. The two fit in the same week, it turns out.

blank_answer: opinions opposite to what I felt first
letter_text: I felt a sting at the news, and by the time I got home I'd built a careful argument for the opposite side. I almost believed myself. I'm beginning to recognize the speed of that flip as a signal — the faster I argue against my first feeling, the more it was probably the true one.

author_pseudonym: the one who says what she thinks
blank_answer: say what I actually think now
letter_text: After a long time of softening every sentence until it lost its shape, I've started saying what I actually mean. Some friendships shrank. The ones that didn't are realer than I knew friendships could be. I'm slower to speak now and I take up more room when I do. It's an even trade.

blank_answer: is harder to talk over now
letter_text: I used to lose the end of my sentences in meetings. Now, more often than not, I finish them. It isn't comfortable. People are surprised. So am I. The difference it makes — in what gets decided, in how I feel walking home — is bigger than I expected.

blank_answer: name what I want without softening it
letter_text: I told my partner this week, plainly, what I needed. No long preamble, no apology before the sentence. He looked surprised, then relieved. I had been hinting at it for months. He had been trying to read the hints and getting it wrong. The plain sentence cost less than the months of hinting did.

blank_answer: watch myself gently, like a friend
letter_text: I notice when I'm tired before I crash. I notice when I'm pretending. I notice the second drink, the avoided call, the way my voice shifts when I'm afraid. I try not to make it a verdict — just a weather report. It keeps me from becoming a stranger to myself.

blank_answer: a notebook for noticing
letter_text: Three lines a night. What happened. What I felt. What I think I was actually feeling. The third line is usually the most honest, and the one I would not have found without the first two. I've kept it for two years now. It's the closest thing I have to a friend who knows everything.

blank_answer: the small things I do under pressure
letter_text: Under pressure my voice goes higher, I avoid certain calls, I reach for sugar at midnight. I used to be embarrassed by these patterns. Now I read them like a mirror, plainly. Knowing them makes them smaller. Knowing them earlier makes them smaller still.

blank_answer: swing from love to hate in a day
letter_text: I keep cycling through people — someone is either the only one who gets me or someone I'm sure never cared. By Wednesday they're a saint, by Friday a stranger. I'm trying to find the middle ground and stay there longer. So far the middle ground feels boring, which I'm starting to think is the point.

blank_answer: tends toward all in or all out
letter_text: I'm either fully invested or already gone. I see people as either rare and good or ones I should never have trusted. I know that's not how people actually are. They're mostly mixed, mostly trying. I'm slowly learning to live in the mostly, which is roomier than the extremes ever were.

blank_answer: rewrite people overnight
letter_text: After one hard night, my steady partner becomes someone I never really knew. By morning he is back to being the man who makes my coffee. I don't tell him about the rewriting; I'm not proud of it. The ones who can wait through that storm get to stay. He's been patient. I'm grateful, quietly.

blank_answer: put hard feelings into a project
letter_text: I turn grief into a project, anger into a long run, longing into a song no one else will hear. Not to escape the feelings — they're still in there — but to give them somewhere to go besides another person's lap. The work is better for it. I am, too, on the days I remember to feel the feeling first.

blank_answer: an old hurt that became patience
letter_text: Something I carried for a long time has slowly turned into patience with difficult clients and care with small details. I don't think the hurt is gone. I think it found a job. Some days I miss its sharpness, which is a strange thing to write down. Mostly I'm relieved.

blank_answer: the garden after a hard week
letter_text: I keep going back to the garden after a hard week. Whatever didn't get said in the meeting goes into the soil. By the end of an afternoon I'm tired in my body and quiet in my head, and the tomatoes don't know any of it. I keep thinking that's a good arrangement.

author_pseudonym: the one who washes the car
blank_answer: do small kind things instead of saying sorry
letter_text: I left a sharp word with my brother last weekend. Since then I've washed his car, cleaned his fridge, sent him an article he'd like — everything except the actual apology. He keeps thanking me. I keep waiting for the moment to say what I'm actually doing. The errands feel easier than the sentence.

blank_answer: check every light I turned off, twice
letter_text: I went back to the kitchen three times tonight to make sure the stove was off. Each time it was. The checking isn't really about the stove. It's a small ritual that keeps something else at bay — I'm not sure what yet. I'm trying to ask the question instead of doing the loop.

author_pseudonym: the one who follows up sweetly
blank_answer: send a kind note right after a sharp email
letter_text: I sent a sharp email this morning and a sweet one in the afternoon, hoping the second would erase the first. It didn't. The recipient was confused, then quietly hurt. I'm learning that the better move is sometimes just naming what I did, plainly, instead of trying to balance it with kindness.
